name: What is the difference between a work injury and an occupational disease?

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between a work injury and an occupational disease?

acceptedAnswer.text: An injury is a sudden event, while an occupational disease is a condition like hearing loss or asbestosis that develops over months or years of exposure. Identifying it as a "disease" claim changes the legal standards and deadlines you must follow.
acceptedAnswer.description: Occupational diseases differ from regular injuries because they develop over time due to repeated exposure rather than a single event. To win, you must prove "legal causation" (the job caused the condition) and "medical causation" (the condition is a recognized disease linked to that exposure). In Salt Lake City and throughout Utah, the "Occupational Disease Act" governs these claims. You need a medical expert to confirm your hearing loss, carpal tunnel, or cancer isn't just a result of aging or outside hobbies. The biggest hurdle is the timeframe; since these diseases manifest slowly, the insurer will claim you're just "getting older." We combat this by documenting your specific years of exposure and industry standards.
